edge: Neural networks are optimized with loss functions containing high-dimensional and high-order differential operators.

Back-propagation is used to evaluate neural networks with high-dimensional and high-order differential operators, but it is expensive due to the scaling of the derivative tensor size and computation graph.

The computation graph has O(2k−1L) scaling, while the derivative tensor size scales with O(dk), where d is the dimension of the domain, L is the number of ops in the forward computation graph, and k is the derivative order.
The size of the derivative tensor scales with the dimension of the domain in the computation graph.
Polynomial scaling in domain was addressed by amortizing the computation over the optimization process via randomization.
The exponential scaling in derivative order for univariate functions was addressed with high-order auto-differentiation.
Efficient contraction of the derivative tensor of arbitrary order for multivariate functions is achieved by constructing input tangents to univariate high-order auto-differentiation.
The method provides >1000× speed-up and >30× memory reduction over randomization with first-order auto-differentiation when applied to Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs).
The method can solve 1-million-dimensional PDEs in 8 minutes on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, opening the possibility of using high-order differential operators in large-scale problems.

In an AD framework, a neural network denoted as Fθ : Rd → Rd′ is constructed for first-order auto-differentiation.
A neural network in an AD framework is constructed as the composition of primitives, which are parameterized by certain parameters.
Each primitives Fi is linearized as the Fréchet derivative, which computes the Jacobian-vector-product.
Each primitives Fi form a linearized computation graph that computes the JVP of the composition.
The linearized computation graph computes the JVP calls, which in turn computes one column of the Jacobian DF.
a is referred to as the primal and v the tangent in the Fi primitives.
By setting the tangent to v as one of the standard basis of Rd, JVP computes one column of the Jacobian DF.

The concept of high-order AD can be defined by analyzing how the high-order Taylor expansion of a univariate function changes when mapped by primitives.
The Fréchet derivative and the univariate chain rule are closely related as shown through their operations on space curves passing through certain points.
The tangent bundle of F can be associated with the first-order expansion of g, which resides in that bundle when considering F as a smooth map between manifolds.
The kth order expansion of the input curve g involves considering multiple derivatives up to the kth order, forming the k-jet of g in the kth order tangent bundle of F.
kth order tangent bundle is defined as the kth-order pushforward of F, represented as dkF :dkF. It pushes the kth order expansion of g to the kth order expansion of F ◦ g.
kth order tangent bundle contains all information needed to evaluate ∂j∂tj [F ◦ g](t) for any j ≤ k. The map dkF is well-defined. dk defines a high-order automatic differentiation.
kth order pushforward pushes the kth order expansion of g to the kth order expansion of F ◦ g. It is represented as ∂tk [F ◦ g](t) and is the k-th order Fréchet derivative.
The high-order univariate chain rule, represented by ∂kF, is known as Faa di Bruno’s formula (Eq. 43). It is used to push the Kth order expansion of g to the kth order expansion of F ◦ g.

We can compute dkF of arbitrary composition F from the kth-order pushforward of the primitives dkFi, since dk is an homomorphism of the group ({Fi},◦). This approach of composing dk of primitives is also known as the Taylor mode AD.

We show how to construct STDE with the pushforward of sparse random jets in section 4.3. The classical method of Hutchinson trace estimator (HTE) [16] can be implemented in the STDE framework as the pushforward of isotropic dense random jets.
It is possible to construct STDE with dense random jets, i.e. jets with tangents that are not the standard basis.
We generalize the dense construction to arbitrary second-order differential operators using a multivariate Gaussian distribution.
STDE can be constructed with dense jets in the case of specific high-order operators like the Biharmonic operator, as shown in Appendix J.

The main differences between sparse and dense versions of STDE are: 1. sparse STDE is universally applicable while dense STDE can only be applied to certain operators, 2. the source of variance is different as mentioned in Appendix K.3.
Both sparse and dense versions of STDE would have similar computation costs if the batch size of random jets were the same.
In general, it is suggested to use sparse STDE unless it is known a priori that the sparse version would suffer from excess variance and the dense STDE is applicable.
